content.text: SLURM as the HPC Batch Scheduler for LLMs

SLURM (Simple Linux Utility for Resource Management) is the dominant workload manager in high-performance computing and research clusters. It predates the Kubernetes era but remains widely used for LLM training because it was built for exactly the pattern those workloads have: long-running multi-node jobs that need exclusive access to GPUs, low-latency InfiniBand interconnects, and a shared parallel filesystem.
The central controller daemon running on the head node. Maintains cluster state, schedules jobs, enforces policies, and talks to slurmd on each compute node.
The node daemon running on every compute node. Receives job steps from slurmctld, launches processes, enforces cgroups-based isolation, and reports resource availability.
Named pools of nodes with shared scheduling policy (time limit, GPU type, preemptability). A job is submitted to a partition; the scheduler assigns it to nodes within that partition.
SLURM's mechanism for tracking non-CPU resources. GPUs appear as
(8 A100s). The GRES plugin binds the correct
devices to each job via cgroups.
Policies that override partition defaults — higher priority, preemption capability, longer time limits, or reserved node access.
A single sbatch submission that launches N independent tasks (

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=llama-pretrain
#SBATCH --partition=gpu
#SBATCH --nodes=4                        # 4 nodes
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=8             # 8 processes per node (one per GPU)
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:a100:8               # 8 A100s per node (32 total)
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8               # CPU threads per process (for DataLoader)
#SBATCH --mem=320G                      # RAM per node
#SBATCH --time=72:00:00                 # wall-clock limit
#SBATCH --output=logs/pretrain_%j.out   # %j = SLURM_JOB_ID
#SBATCH --error=logs/pretrain_%j.err
#SBATCH --mail-type=FAIL,END
#SBATCH --mail-user=researcher@org.edu

# Load environment modules (cluster-managed software stacks)
module load cuda/12.2
module load nccl/2.18
module load openmpi/4.1.5

# Activate conda environment from shared NFS
source /scratch/envs/llm-train/bin/activate

# NCCL tuning for InfiniBand
export NCCL_IB_DISABLE=0
export NCCL_IB_HCA=mlx5_0            # InfiniBand HCA interface
export NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL=2           # GPU Direct RDMA
export NCCL_DEBUG=WARN
export CUDA_DEVICE_ORDER=PCI_BUS_ID

# torchrun rendezvous via SLURM-provided environment variables
export MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames "$SLURM_JOB_NODELIST" | head -n 1)
export MASTER_PORT=29500

srun torchrun \
    --nnodes=$SLURM_NNODES \
    --nproc_per_node=$SLURM_NTASKS_PER_NODE \
    --rdzv_id=$SLURM_JOB_ID \
    --rdzv_backend=c10d \
    --rdzv_endpoint=${MASTER_ADDR}:${MASTER_PORT} \
    train.py \
        --model meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf \
        --data /scratch/datasets/pile \
        --checkpoint-dir /scratch/checkpoints/llama7b-$SLURM_JOB_ID \
        --global-batch-size 1024 \
        --max-steps 100000
prefix is critical: it launches the job as a SLURM step, giving SLURM visibility into individual process health. If a process dies, SLURM can detect and optionally restart the step.
then handles DDP process group initialisation within the

# Allocate an interactive GPU session (useful for debugging)
srun --partition=gpu --gres=gpu:a100:1 --cpus-per-task=8 \
     --mem=64G --time=4:00:00 --pty bash

# Inside the session:
nvidia-smi      # verify GPU allocation
python3 -c "import torch; print(torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))"

# For multi-GPU interactive debugging:
srun --partition=gpu --nodes=2 --ntasks-per-node=4 \
     --gres=gpu:a100:4 --pty bash

# Queue status
squeue --me --format="%.10i %.9P %.30j %.8u %.8T %.10M %.6D %R"
# JOBID  PARTITION   NAME               USER     STATE       TIME  NODES REASON
# 12345  gpu         llama-pretrain     alice    RUNNING  3:14:02      4

# Detailed job info
scontrol show job 12345

# Check GPU utilisation on running nodes
srun --jobid=12345 --overlap nvidia-smi

# View accounting for completed jobs
sacct -j 12345 --format=JobID,JobName,AllocTRES,Elapsed,State,ExitCode

# Real-time log tail
tail -f logs/pretrain_12345.out

# Node state summary
sinfo --format="%20N %6c %8m %10G %5t"
# NODELIST             CPUS   MEMORY   GRES       STATE
# gpu-node-[01-02]     96     320000+  gpu:a100:8 alloc
# gpu-node-[03-04]     96     320000+  gpu:a100:8 idle
SLURM jobs are killed when the
limit is reached or a node fails. Robust training scripts must checkpoint regularly and resume cleanly on restart.

#!/bin/bash
# train.py — checkpoint-aware training loop
import torch, os
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, Trainer, TrainingArguments

CHECKPOINT_DIR = os.environ.get("CHECKPOINT_DIR", "/scratch/checkpoints")
JOB_ID = os.environ.get("SLURM_JOB_ID", "local")

args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir=f"{CHECKPOINT_DIR}/{JOB_ID}",
    save_strategy="steps",
    save_steps=500,            # checkpoint every 500 steps
    save_total_limit=3,        # keep only last 3 checkpoints
    resume_from_checkpoint=True,  # auto-detect last checkpoint in output_dir
    logging_steps=50,
    bf16=True,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")
trainer = Trainer(model=model, args=args, train_dataset=train_ds)

# Detects existing checkpoint in output_dir automatically
trainer.train(resume_from_checkpoint=args.resume_from_checkpoint)

# sbatch wrapper that resubmits itself if training hasn't converged
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=llama-pretrain-auto
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00         # resubmit every 24h

CHECKPOINT_DIR="/scratch/checkpoints/llama7b"
DONE_MARKER="$CHECKPOINT_DIR/TRAINING_COMPLETE"

if [ -f "$DONE_MARKER" ]; then
    echo "Training already complete; not resubmitting."
    exit 0
fi

# Run training (will resume from checkpoint if one exists)
srun python3 train.py --checkpoint-dir "$CHECKPOINT_DIR"

EXIT_CODE=$?
if [ $EXIT_CODE -eq 0 ]; then
    touch "$DONE_MARKER"
else
    # Resubmit — scheduler will start us again after job completion
    sbatch "$0"
fi

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=hp-sweep
#SBATCH --array=0-19%4            # 20 tasks, max 4 concurrent
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:a100:1
#SBATCH --time=8:00:00

# Map array index to hyperparameters
LR_VALUES=(1e-5 2e-5 5e-5 1e-4)
BS_VALUES=(8 16 32)

LR=${LR_VALUES[$((SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID % 4))]}
BS=${BS_VALUES[$((SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID / 4 % 3))]}
SEED=$((SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID * 42))

echo "Task $SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID: lr=$LR bs=$BS seed=$SEED"

python3 finetune.py \
    --lr "$LR" \
    --batch-size "$BS" \
    --seed "$SEED" \
    --output-dir "/scratch/sweeps/run_${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}"
SLURM Partitions and QOS for LLM Workloads
A well-configured cluster typically has multiple partitions for different workload types. Common patterns in LLM research clusters:

# QOS definitions (configured by cluster admin in sacctmgr)
sacctmgr add qos high-priority \
    MaxTRESPerUser=gpu=32 \
    Priority=1000 \
    GrpTRES=gpu=64

sacctmgr add qos preemptable \
    Priority=0 \
    Flags=DenyOnLimit,Preemptable

# Grant QOS to a user or account
sacctmgr modify user alice set qos+=high-priority
sacctmgr modify account research set qos+=preemptable

# Submit with QOS override
sbatch --qos=high-priority --partition=gpu job.sh
LLM training across SLURM nodes uses either MPI (OpenMPI/MPICH via PMIx) or PyTorch's
with c10d rendezvous. The choice affects how process groups are bootstrapped:
Rank 0 acts as rendezvous server on MASTER_ADDR:MASTER_PORT; other ranks connect
PyTorch DDP/FSDP, DeepSpeed — most LLM training
SLURM launches processes via PMIx; OpenMPI handles all-reduce
Legacy MPI training, Megatron-LM with MPI collectives
NCCL reads SLURM env vars for ring topology; UCX/IB transport
Implicit in both above — NCCL uses IB RDMA automatically

# Minimal torchrun setup using SLURM environment variables
# In train.py (called from sbatch via srun torchrun):
import os, torch
import torch.distributed as dist

def init_distributed():
    rank = int(os.environ["RANK"])
    local_rank = int(os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"])
    world_size = int(os.environ["WORLD_SIZE"])

    torch.cuda.set_device(local_rank)

    dist.init_process_group(
        backend="nccl",          # GPU-to-GPU via NVLink / IB RDMA
        rank=rank,
        world_size=world_size,
    )
    return rank, local_rank, world_size

rank, local_rank, world_size = init_distributed()
# torchrun sets RANK, LOCAL_RANK, WORLD_SIZE from torchrun args
# which srun derives from SLURM_PROCID, SLURM_LOCALID, SLURM_NTASKS
to request different resource specs for different job components. For LLM workloads this enables a
pattern where the coordinator runs on a CPU node and workers on GPU nodes:

#!/bin/bash
# Heterogeneous job: 1 CPU coordinator + 4 GPU workers
#SBATCH --job-name=llm-hj
# Component 0: coordinator
#SBATCH packjob
#SBATCH --nodes=1 --ntasks=1 --partition=cpu

# Component 1: GPU workers
#SBATCH packjob
#SBATCH --nodes=4 --ntasks-per-node=8 --partition=gpu --gres=gpu:a100:8

srun --het-group=0 python3 coordinator.py &
srun --het-group=1 torchrun --nnodes=4 --nproc_per_node=8 worker.py
wait

# Submit a job
sbatch job.sh

# List my jobs
squeue --me

# Cancel a job
scancel 12345

# Cancel all my jobs
scancel --user=$USER

# Hold / release a pending job
scontrol hold 12345
scontrol release 12345

# Node status
sinfo --partition=gpu --format="%20N %5t %8c %8m %10G"

# Drain a node (admin: no new jobs)
scontrol update NodeName=gpu-node-03 State=DRAIN Reason="GPU ECC error"

# Resume a drained node
scontrol update NodeName=gpu-node-03 State=RESUME

# Job accounting (costs, time used)
sacct --user=$USER --starttime=2025-06-01 \
      --format=JobID,JobName,AllocTRES,Elapsed,CPUTimeRAW,State

# Estimate start time for a pending job
squeue --me --start

run on the head node of the allocation, not the compute nodes. Always prefix multi-node distributed commands with
doesn't match the actual InfiniBand HCA name (check
), NCCL silently falls back to TCP, making all-reduce 10-100× slower. Verify with
and look for 'IB transport' in the log.
A 70B model on 32 GPUs might take 40 hours to train. Request 20% more than the estimate to avoid SIGTERM mid-training. Most schedulers send SIGUSR1 at
to allow graceful checkpoint before the kill.
Tokenising a 1 TB dataset on NFS from 32 processes simultaneously causes metadata storms. Use a dedicated preprocessing step on the CPU partition first, writing to Lustre's high-throughput OSTs.
GPU MIG not exposed to jobs:
MIG slices must be configured by the admin and added to the GRES config (
; the SLURM GRES plugin binds the correct device file.
. Activate conda/virtualenv inside the sbatch script explicitly, before srun, not inside a
SLURM is the standard workload manager for multi-node LLM training on HPC clusters. Its core model — sbatch scripts requesting GPUs via

to launch distributed steps — maps directly to torchrun-based DDP and FSDP training. Job arrays handle hyperparameter sweeps; QOS policies control preemption and priority. SLURM lacks native autoscaling and inference serving, making it most appropriate for training and evaluation workloads where Kubernetes and Ray fill the serving gap.
